relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: The Eternal appeared by the oaks of Mamre while Abraham sat in the heat of the day.
Abraham lifted his eyes and saw three visitors, and hospitality rose in him immediately.
He asked the visitor not to pass by, offering welcome from the place of favor.
Water, washed feet, and rest under the tree became the first acts of sacred welcome.
Bread was offered to strengthen the heart before the journey continued.
Abraham hurried to Sarah, and the household moved quickly to prepare nourishment.
He chose what was tender and good and gave it to be prepared.
The meal was set before the visitors, and Abraham stood by as they ate under the tree.
They asked for Sarah by name, drawing her into the center of the promise.
The promise returned: Sarah would have a son at the time of life, while she listened from the tent.
Age and the ceased way of women made the promise impossible by ordinary measure.
Sarah laughed within herself, her body and honesty meeting the absurdity of renewed delight.
The Eternal named the hidden laughter and brought it into the open.
Nothing is too wonderful for the Eternal; the impossible promise had an appointed time.
Sarah denied her laughter because fear came over her, but the truth was gently named.
The visitors turned toward Sodom, and Abraham walked with them toward the next revelation.
The Eternal considered whether to hide the coming judgment from Abraham.
Abraham’s vocation was blessing for nations, so he was drawn into the question of justice.
His household was called to keep the way of the Eternal through righteousness and justice.
The outcry of Sodom and Gomorrah had become great, and their offense was heavy.
The Eternal came down to see, showing that judgment listens to the cry before it acts.
The messengers went toward Sodom while Abraham remained before the Eternal.
Abraham drew near and challenged indiscriminate destruction: will the righteous be swept away with the harmful?
He began with fifty, pleading that a righteous presence might preserve the place.
He appealed to the deepest truth: the Judge of all the earth must do justice.
The Eternal accepted the principle: for fifty righteous, the whole place would be spared.
Abraham continued humbly, calling himself dust and ashes while daring to intercede.
He moved from fifty to forty-five, testing the wideness of mercy.
He asked for forty, and mercy still answered.
He asked for thirty, careful before the Divine fire of justice.
He asked for twenty, continuing to press mercy into judgment.
He asked for ten, and even ten righteous would have held the city back from ruin.
The conversation ended, and Abraham returned to his place carrying the weight of intercession.
